[[RunningHead]]

Hitler's Psychopathology

By Norbert Bromberg and Verna Volz Small. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, 335 pp., $30.00.

Donald Kaplan Ph.D.

Donald Kaplan

Donald M. Kaplan, Ph.D.
New York City

Donald Kaplan  

It is difficult to imagine an end to the reasons for our fascination with the biography, personality, and career of Adolph Hitler. We still trace much of our present political life to the rise and fall of Hitler's long moment of uniquely ruthless and irresponsible power—the divisions of Europe and other global spheres, international decolonization, the existence of Israel, even the nuclear arms race. Moreover, Hitler's methods of terrorism, torture, and carnage are the immediate comparisons with fanatical events, often racial and religious, that continue to claim by repression and slaughter ordinary human lives all over the planet. As Bromberg and Small rightly observe early on in their book, it is for such reasons as these that the subject of Hitler is far from dead despite the abundance of books about it, particularly since the 1970's.

On the other hand, if the huge historical industry of the past several decades has exhausted the biographical data on Hitler's life and conduct, on what can another psychoanalytic study of Hitler be advanced? It is Bromberg and Small's contention that much of such data can be reworked by recent developments of psychoanalytic thought itself. The developments they have in mind are diagnostic, which entail also ego functions and sexuality. Thus Hitler's Psychopathology embodies a perspective of borderline and narcissistic issues that have arisen from the psychoanalytic contributions of O. F.

759

Blood Brothers: Siblings as Writers

Edited by Norman Kiell. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, XIII + 434 pp., $40.00.

Jesse Schomer M.D.

Jesse Schomer

Jesse Schomer, M.D.
White Plains, New York

Jesse Schomer  

How many of the enduring stories of our western world—its Greek and Roman legends, the stark tales of the Old Testament, and the fables collected from central Europe by the Brothers Grimm—deal with the vicissitudes of love and hate between brothers? Combining this interest in prototypical siblings with the intrinsic appeal in the lives and works of creative artists, Norman Kiell has selected an immensely attractive and unique category of subjects for this fascinating, ambitious, and flawed book: a dozen pairs of brothers, each a writer, at least one of whom has achieved a position of distinction, and, in several instances, towering eminence in modern literature. Felicitously conceived, but desultorily edited, the book attempts to demonstrate the validity of two closely linked hypotheses: that the brothers' sibling relationship was a major determinant of the course of their artistic lives; and that the psychological derivatives of this relationship were reflected in the central themes and main characters of their literary works.

Tales of Sex and Violence

By Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1985, 145 pp., $16.95.

John Ross Ph.D.

John Ross

John Munder Ross, Ph.D.
New York City

John Ross  

Psychoanalysis has not had an easy time of late with Sanskritists seeking to embrace or employ our discipline's precepts. Fortunately, in Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty, we have found a most congenial collaborator. Although she has not received formal analytic training, in her work Dr. O'Flaherty has applied analytic ideas and modes of interpretation to a mythology that is over three thousand years in the making—one that enriches our lore in turn, with its ample testimony to the workings of primary process and to the human condition.

The successor to Mircea Eliade at the University of Chicago, where she is professor of religion and Southeast Asian Studies and a member of the Committee on Social Thought, Dr. O'Flaherty has published in the last 12 years a half-dozen volumes collecting and explicating the major Sanskrit texts. Tales of Sex and Violence is the last to date in this series and perhaps the slimmest of her volumes, a brief book, whose avowed purpose is the resuscitation and exegesis of the little-known and hitherto even less respected Jaiminiya Brahmana, written roughly around 900 B.C.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Sense of Defect

Jennifer Downey M.D.

Jennifer Downey

Jennifer Downey  

May 28, 1986

I am writing in response to Dr. Coen's (1986) very interesting article published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association (34:47-67).

The sense of defect is a useful concept in work with patients with a variety of defects, as Dr. Coen points out. The fantasies a patient develops with the defect as a nidus are of great importance whether the defect is a physical or emotional one, visible to others or not, limiting capacity or life-span or not.

However, Dr. Coen elects to deal with all these defects as psychological only—a fantasy—and then proceeds to discuss the motivations for the persistence of the fantasy. This is an oversimplification which by encompassing so many kinds of defect is confusing rather than helpful. Further, the suggestion that we view the disability or defect as just a fantasy is misleading. A patient's body may impose limitations that must be dealt with just as the mind does. By analogy, we do not consider level of cognitive ability a fantasy, although many fantasies may be elaborated about it and inhibitions may make it seem less than it is. Whether or not one has a physical appearance normal enough not to attract attention is important, just as it is important whether or not one has a basic capacity like the ability to see or to walk. It is particularly important that we as analysts consciously endeavor not to deny abnormalities of capacity and appearance, because they can be so painful that it is quite tempting to do so.

I would like to offer an illustration from my own practice. I work in a medical center where I treat patients with a variety of serious physical problems including genetic abnormalities, disorders of sexual differentiation, and the like. A patient with dwarfism consulted me for help with social withdrawal of many years' standing. In order to be able to visit my office, she obtained a driver's license and a specially equipped car. She then made a test run and found that she could not park the car, since she was so short she could not reach the ticket dispenser in the automated parking garage, and there was no alternative parking available. Unable to obtain a parking place for her myself, despite vigorous efforts, I shared the patient's sense of futility and outrage. Here I was forced to see the real limitations with which this patient lived on a daily basis, but it was quite aversive to do so since I shared her helplessness.

[[RunningHead]]

Adult Development: A New Dimension in Psychodynamic Theory and Practice

By Calvin A. Colarusso and Robert Nemiroff. New York: Plenum Press, 1981, 290 pp., $24.50.

Alexander Broden M.D.

Alexander Broden

Alexander R. Broden, M.D.
Scarsdale, NY

Alexander Broden  

Drs. Colarusso and Nemiroff have attempted to collate their understanding of the works of past and current theorists on adult development and present it to a wide audience comprising both professionals and intelligent laymen.

In an opening chapter, the authors carefully lay out the basic tenets of their work. They emphasize that adult developmental processes continue throughout one's life span. Furthermore, they point out, although childhood developmental themes continue to play a central role in the adult, too little attention has been paid to the effects of adult experience on mental development.

The first part of the book is devoted to a review of theories of adulthood and evaluation in the writings of ancient Greek philosophers, Confucius, Japanese spiritualism and Christianity, followed by an outline of the writings of pioneer "developmentalists" such as the anthropologist A. Van Gennep, Freud, Jung, and Erikson whose delineations of the eight stages of life seems to be highly influential in the authors' writings. Their rather superficial reviews go on to outline the work of recent theorists such as D. J. Levinson, G. Vaillant, R. Gould, and B. Neugarten, and finally some of the more important relevant works of George Pollock, who suggests that the mourning process leads to new forms of creativity in older individuals.

The second part of the book is concerned with a systematic development of the authors' own concepts. Here the authors begin to falter with a reductionistic viewpoint they initially purport to avoid. For example, they present a group of sweeping generalizations such as: (1) the nature of the developmental process is basically the same in the adult as in the child; (2) the adult is just as dependent on his environment as is the child; (3) the fundamental developmental issues of childhood continue as central aspects of adult life; (4) development in adulthood is deeply influenced by physical change. However, they fail to present sufficient case material or controlled studies which would be of major importance in testing their hypothesis, leaving us with little more than general descriptions of dependence and biological change in adulthood.

[[RunningHead]]

In Freud's Shadow—Adler in Context

By Paul E. Stepansky. Hillsdale, N.J.: Analytic Press, 1983, 325 pp., $29.95.

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein, Ph.D.
Los Angeles, California

Rudolf Ekstein  

Paul Stepansky, a brilliant historian, has given us a beautiful book on the old controversy about and between Adler and Freud, a discussion not only in terms of theoretical differences, but written carefully and concisely against the historical background of their time. He writes with a thorough knowledge of the cultural milieu, the political changes and tensions from 1906, when Adler joined Freud and his group, to the breakdown of the Austrian Republic and the slow destruction of the democratic society through Fascism.

I cannot help but read the book and report about it through the eyes of someone who was there, who knew the political leaders of the time, the educators, the circles around Adler and Freud, our teachers at the University and the political life at that time. It is difficult to conceive that a man brought up in the United States could read and live himself into European intellectual history as if he had been there, and could enable me to return to this era with his book as a guide.

As I look at this title, I cannot help but wonder whether Alfred Adler stood in Freud's shadow or, whether he needed Freud, who was 14 years older than he, to grow away from, to get out of the shadow, and to be a creative man himself. Stepansky convincingly describes the struggle between Adler and Freud, the conflict within Adler to free himself from the master, to get out of his shadow, a simile Adler once used. But there were other creative contributory psychoanalysts at that time, and they did not need to leave Freud, and did not become blind disciples.

757

[[RunningHead]]

Falling Off the Couch

Richard Waugaman M.D.

Richard Waugaman

Richard M. Waugaman, M.D.
Chestnut Lodge
500 West Montgomery Ave.
Rockville, MD 20850

Richard Waugaman  

ABSTRACT

The patient's therapeutic regression intensifies certain unconscious meanings of the analytic couch. In addition to representing the analysis or the analyst in general, the couch can represent the unconscious, or it may take on the symbolic significance of the analyst's or mother's arms, lap, breasts, or womb. When the genetic roots of the patient's transference include substantial experiences of disappointment, narcissistic injury, and mistrust, the theme of falling from the couch may emerge as a dream, an association, or even an enactment. This theme usually implies the presence of a deepening but mistrustful transference, based on earlier disappointments by the patient's primary objects. Falling off the couch may be associated with being dropped as an infant, rolling out of bed as a child, birth, miscarriage, castration, death, termination, defending against passive wishes, punishment for sexual or aggressive transgressions, escaping an attack, descending into the unconscious, or wanting to be picked up and comforted.

responseSet.title: Reply to Jennifer Downey
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Jennifer Downey

Stanley Coen M.D.

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen  

Dr. Downey is concerned that physical disability or defect not be regarded as just a fantasy. She warns, appropriately, of the analyst's countertransference potential for denial of the painfulness and severity of patient's physical abnormalities. I agree completely with her. Either/or understanding of concepts can lead to oversimplification. Dr. Downey misunderstands my position as one of oversimplification—that physical defects are to be regarded as only psychological, ignoring or minimizing the actual physical defect. Exclusive emphasis on either extreme, the actual physical reality or only inner fantasy, would miss the interplay and integration we psychoanalysts seek between external reality and intrapsychic meanings and constructions.

I do not want to be misunderstood as implying that the sense of defect is a compromise formation unrelated to, or minimizing patients' bodily reality. Bearing in mind fully such bodily reality, I emphasized the usefulness of the concept of sense of defect as a compromise formation crucial for the progress of an analysis, that is, as something to be understood and analyzed. My case material, unlike Dr. Downey's, included patients who once had severe physical abnormalities or defects, which now had far less "actual" impact on their lives, and patients with "realistically trivial" defects who, nevertheless, felt themselves to be defective.

Sense of defectiveness had not, to my knowledge, been written about in detail previously. My aim was to understand this sense of defectiveness psychoanalytically and to consider how to analyze it. The opposite countertransference risk, of collusion with the patient that the feelings of defectiveness are realistic or appropriate, can minimize the patient's capacity for growth out of severe feelings of defectiveness. We certainly do need to maintain balance in our perspectives toward bodily reality and its limitations and the possibilities for better resolution of neurotic conflict. Also, when the actual trauma (defect, injury, illness, surgery, ridicule, rejection, etc.) has been too deeply etched, the potential for intrapsychic change may be limited and disappointing.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatric Clinics of North America: On Eating Disorders

Edited by Félix E. F. Larocca. Philadelphia: Saunders, 1984, 407 pp., $21.95.

Lawrence Deutsch M.D.

Lawrence Deutsch

Lawrence Deutsch, M.D.
Englewood, New Jersey

Lawrence Deutsch  

This volume is reflective of the current biopsychosocial attitudes toward psychiatric and psychosomatic illness. It is remarkable to read an entire volume with but two comments about psychoanalytic research on anorexia nervosa, bulimia, and obesity. It is even more remarkable to have this volume espouse Minuchin's family therapy, behavior modification, psychopharmacology and self-help groups, while including only a brief note by one of the authors that psychoanalytic workers have also investigated these illnesses.

The authors thus attempt to find genetic and experiential factors which either predispose to or result in endocrine, metabolic, or other physiologic dysfunction. The recommended therapeutic interventions are pharmacological and/or psychosocial, with emphasis on behavioral therapies.

Psychiatric Clinics, not withstanding its particular bias, has something important to offer analysts and psychotherapists who treat patients with eating disorders. Clearly delineated are the myriad research endeavors involving endocrine, metabolic, genetic, and environmental factors. Also described are the many therapeutic approaches, some derived from research findings, some empirical. In addition, the volume explores anorexia, bulimia, and obesity as separate entities, descriptively biologically, and therapeutically. A brief comment about the findings presented for each of these groups may demonstrate the relevance of these data for those doing analysis or analytically oriented psychotherapy with these patients.

Primary anorexia nervosa, as defined in the chapter by R. Munoz, refers to patients who have "no other known psychiatric disorder." These patients must show (1) "a distorted implacable attitude toward eating, food, or weight, that overrides hunger, admonitions, reassurance and threats"; (2) an age of onset prior to 25 years; (3) anorexia with accompanying weight loss of at least 25% of body weight; (4) evidence of typical manifestations of anorexia nervosa: "amenorrhea, lanugo, bradycardia, periods of overactivity, episodes of bulimia, and vomiting (may be self-induced).

[[RunningHead]]

Idealization of the Analyst by the Young Adult

Judith Chused M.D.

Judith Chused

Judith Fingert Chused, M.D.
1805 Randolph St., N.W.
Washington, DC 20011

Judith Chused  

ABSTRACT

Idealization is an intrapsychic process that serves many functions. In addition to its use defensively and for gratification of libidinal and aggressive drive derivatives, it can contribute to developmental progression, particularly during late adolescence and young adulthood. During an analysis, it is important to recognize all the determinants of idealization, including those related to the reworking of developmental conflicts. If an analyst understands idealization solely as a manifestation of pathology, he may interfere with his patient's use of it for the development of autonomous functioning.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference

Edgar Lipton M.D.

Edgar Lipton

Edgar Lipton  

September 4, 1986

After reading the excellent series of papers on the topic of countertransference in Volume 34, Number 2 of the Journal, I would like to offer a few points which, to my knowledge, have been written about very little.

Patients come to every therapist, no matter the school, with ready transferences. The Balints, Winnicott, and Leo Stone have written of the basic transference of a supportive mother figure. However, the possibilities of early countertransferences before the patient arrives have not been addressed. The patient making an appointment can be influenced, for example, by the referral source. What the patient has been told about the therapist of his background, whether the appointment is made solely because of the patient's distress and/or under duress from employer, spouse, school, or court, makes a difference. Each will influence attitudes and fantasy formation.

The accepting analyst also has attitudes and fantasies. What will the patient be like—a manageable neurotic or a potentially violent and unpredictable psychotic, etc. The source of referral creates pressures—a referral from one's old therapist, a colleague whom one identifies with or despises, or one with whom one is very competitive Does the patient come from an untrustworthy colleague? Is the patient rich, powerful, or well-known to the public? All are potentially disrupting. Then there are the possibilities or prejudices from what one knows in advance about the patient, especially from the unconscious anxieties that come from knowledge of the presence of physical disease and/or disfigurement. Presumptive diagnosis of a perversion may induce a tendency to make an advance moral judgment.

The necessity of a successful treatment of a neurotic patient for graduation from an institute is in a class with the influence of third persons—supervisor, spouse of the patient, or others whose good will is important to the therapist. All of them may influence attitudes before the patient ever arrives in the consulting room. Interestingly, once the patient-analyst transferences have been set in motion by a period of working together and familiarity, these countertransferences usually drop out. If they do not, of course, it is a time for self-analysis or consultation.

[[RunningHead]]

A Comment on Frank R. Hartman's "A Reappraisal of the Emma Episode and the Specimen Dream"

Henry Cohen

Henry Cohen

Henry Cohen  

February 3, 1986

Frank R. Hartman, in "A Reappraisal of the Emma Episode and the Specimen Dream" (This Journal, 31:555-585), contends that Emma Eckstein, the victim of a nasal operation by Wilhelm Fliess, was not the Irma of Freud's speciman dream. This may be so, but, even if Emma was not Irma, Hartman tries too hard to dissociate the two.

Hartman disagrees with other scholars that the Emma episode even constituted day residue of the Irma dream. Hartman's main argument in this regard apparently is that Emma's massive nasal hemorrhage was in March, 1895, while the Irma dream and Freud's analysis of it occurred in July, 1895. The term "day residue," Hartman suggests, is generally not used to refer to an incident more than several days before the dream to which it contributes. Nevertheless, the subject matters of the Emma episode and the Irma dream are so similar that, as J. N. Isbister says in his recent biography of Freud, "It stretches our credulity to believe that this incident [the Emma episode] did not spring to mind when Freud was presenting his associations [to the Irma dream]."

Hartman also disputes the view that the Emma episode could be considered "current conflict" of the Irma dream. He apparently takes Freud's unawareness of current conflict in connection with his non vixet dream of 1898 as evidence that, if the Emma episode had been a current conflict in connection with the 1895 Irma dream, then Freud might have been unaware that it was. It is unclear how one's unawareness in one situation constitutes any significant evidence that one was unaware in another situation three years earlier.

responseSet.title: Response to Henry Cohen's Comment
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Henry Cohen's Comment

Frank Hartman M.D.

Frank Hartman

Frank Hartman  

For a writer such as is Isbister, it may well stretch his "… credulity to believe that this incident [the Emma episode] did not spring to mind when Freud was presenting his associations [to the Irma dream]." However, working with resistance and the dynamic unconscious produces daily examples of what does not come to mind in free association or dream association. Emma Eckstein's operation and complications were behind Freud and well defended when he had the specimen dream. Thus, it is not surprising that Emma did not enter Freud's associations.

Only Schur argued that the Emma episode was day residue. In support of his idiosyncratic view, Schur cited Fisher's 1954 paper. Apparently, Cohen has joined Schur in either not reading or not understanding Fisher's work.

My argument is that the Emma episode represented an acting out of infantile conflict on the part of Freud and that Freud's (1914) words apply: "… the patient does not remember anything of what he has forgotten and repressed, but acts it out. He reproduces it not as a memory but as an action; he repeats it, without, of course, knowing that he is repeating it" (S.E., 12, p. 150). Only after beginning self-analysis in 1897, did Freud understand the Emma episode and the nature of his relationship with Fliess.

Freud's letters to Fliess during Emma's recovery (March-April, 1895) sought to reassure Fliess about leaving gauze in the wound. Leaving aside the many inconsistencies in Schur's 1966, 1969 and 1972 works, the quotations he used in support of his thesis of exculpation do begin on April 26, 1896, while Freud was blaming Emma for her bleeding rather than Fliess (and himself).

John Gedo

John E. Gedo, M.D.
Chicago, ILL

John Gedo  

Peter Gay, Durfee Professor of History at Yale University, won the 1967 National Book Award for a two-volume study, The Enlightenment, and the R. W. Emerson Award for the authoritative monograph on Weimar Culture. His interest in psychoanalysis became manifest about a decade ago with a book of historiography, Art and Act. On Causes in History—Manet, Gropius, Mondrian, where he argued persuasively for the necessity of giving due weight to the psychological determinants of cultural events. He has been one of the most influential among historians who emphasize the singular importance of Sigmund Freud in shaping the civilization of our times, an estimate proclaimed through the very title of Gay's recent book, Freud, Jews and Other Germans. These intellectual commitments continue to pervade Gay's work, presently focused on the social history of the 19th century bourgeoisie in Western Europe and its American counterparts: Sigmund Freud is once again given star billing in the title of this enormous enterprise.

Education of the Senses is the first volume in a series that will examine the life of the Victorian middle class from a variety of viewpoints; when it was published, the exact organization of the work as a whole had not as yet been decided. It is apparent, however, that we are being presented with a major effort in the new genre of history inaugurated in France some decades ago by the group associated with the journal Annales, the best known among whom is probably Fernand Braudel. The latter entitled one of his capital books The Structures of Everyday Life—a designation that could serve admirably to characterize the program of Gay's current work. Cries of the Wolf Man

By Patrick J. Mahony. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, xii + 185 pp., $20.00.

Darius Ornston M.D.

Darius Ornston

Darius Gray Ornston, M.D.
Greenville, South Carolina

Darius Ornston  

Good students often grumble when told Freud is a great writer. They can see his vivid descriptions and his fantastic development of common ideas. They respect his prodigious life work and his pervasive influence on the ways we all listen and read, heal and learn. But just what is it that makes Freud a great writer?

Patrick Mahony, a practicing analyst and Professor of English literature is studying that question. In his first book, Freud as a Writer (1982), Mahony began to focus his inquiry, assemble evidence, and set up some working hypotheses about Freud's style. Now he is working his way through Freud's major case histories. Mahoney has sorted, investigated, and integrated all available letters, interviews, and "reformulations" about Freud's most famous, and perhaps favorite, patient who is widely known as the Wolf Man. This includes 70 years of revisions and ruminations in German, French, and English.

Psychoanalytic Theory of Art: A Philosophy of Art on Developmental Principles

By Richard Kuhns. New York: Columbia Univ. Press, 1983, xiii + 169 pp., $27.50 (paperback, $13.00).

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
New York, NY

Aaron Esman  

Since Freud's pioneering effort in his Leonardo paper of 1910, psychoanalysts have tried in various ways to apply their methods and insights to the artifacts of culture, to the lives of some of those who have created them, and to the processes through which they have done so. Their labors have met with mixed responses from those who have traditionally regarded themselves as the guardians and interpreters of the cultural corpus—the art historians, critics, and social scientists—responses ranging from disdain and skepticism to guarded or sympathetic acceptance. Strikingly, in recent years these humanist scholars have demonstrated growing interest in the possibilities of intellectual exchange with psychoanalysts and of beneficial exploitation of psychoanalytic ideas.

Nowhere has such interest been expressed more enthusiastically, however, than in this essay by a philosopher of esthetics, which amounts to nothing less than a proposal for the total integration of psychoanalysis into the methods of cultural analysis. In Kuhns' view, every artistic product—he refers to them generically as "objects"—has both an individual and a social, an immediate and a historical dimension. Each is an "enactment," a cultural event that exists within a tradition—or, as he puts it, "culture is a tradition of enactments" (p. 53). Each such "enactment" is susceptible to interpretation along both psychological and historical lines, as both a private and a public event.

New Haven, CT

Lorraine Siggins  

This book is one volume in a series called Women in Context: Development and Stress. Previous volumes have been titled The Woman Patient, Becoming Female, and Women's Sexual Development. This latest volume explicitly sets out to raise questions about women's sexual experience rather than to give answers. While this is a laudable goal, the result is disappointing—a disjointed potpourri of ideas and opinions. Each chapter is written by a different author: journalists, legislative advocates, family service workers, founders of special interest groups, and law enforcement experts, psychologists, sociologists, obstetricians and gynecologists, internists, urologists, sex therapists, and psychiatrists.

The range of topics covered is very wide and the fifteen chapters are divided into two sections. The first five chapters deal with the general condition of women's sexual response and the second part with special circumstances. The topics covered under the general condition, for example, are: women's sexual response, the sexual experience of Afro-American women, sex and sexuality, and women at midlife. The chapters under special circumstances include: incest, female sexuality and pregnancy, voluntary childlessness, alternative sexual patterns, wife abuse, venereal disease, and prostitution.

On Women, Sexuality and Love

By Georg Simmel; translated and with an introduction by Guy Oaks. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1984, 194 pp., $20.00.

Martha Kirkpatrick M.D.

Martha Kirkpatrick

Martha Kirkpatrick, M.D.
Los Angeles, California

Martha Kirkpatrick  

Georg Simmel, a brilliant German philosopher and sociologist of the early twentieth century, sprinkles his pages with golden insights between layers of an arcane, idiosyncratic, muddy philosophical argot. Fortunately, Guy Oaks has provided us with a careful and spirited translation and with a lucid and elucidating introduction. His quarter of the volume not only relates these essays to Simmel's philosophy of

275

culture, but prepares and guides the reader through mazes of otherwise obscure conceptualizations.

The "woman question" and the defining features of the feminine character have been topics of philosophical interest to men throughout recorded history (i.e., male history). Their interest has led to many a quaint and curious theory, usually debasing and blaming women, while elevating and exonerating men. In his essay on Female Culture (1911), Simmel does not follow this custom. Although his often dazzling, hair-splitting, convoluted arguments are unconvincing as proofs, he arrives at remarkably contemporary insights. At a time when it was fashionable to believe that women remain "overgrown children for their entire lives" (Schopenhauer) or represent wanton sexuality and chaos undermining the rational and civilizing achievements of men (Weininger), Simmel saw women as different but not deficient. Simmel argues that "objective" culture is masculine culture, not simply neutral human culture. It is, in fact, an objectification of male interests and values. The first example he presents is "law," pointing out that the female sense of justice is not defective, but simply different from "male law"—thus adumbrating Roy Schafer's criticism of Freud's view of the female superego as defective. Since male law has been defined as the valid "body of law," a body of law that developed from the specifically female sense of justice would not be acknowledged. Here he seems close to Carol Gilligan's thesis. He sees female nature as estranged from this dominant (male) culture and distorted by comparison with the male model for human perfection—very contemporary thinking. Simmel sees clearly what contemporary women, in the throes of identifying their subjugated state, so often fail to comprehend, i.e., entrance into the male value world neither necessarily liberates women nor feminizes the male world, but may create a monosexual culture based on male values. On the other hand, an "autonomous femininity" would be derived from a "completely distinctive foundation that is neither shared with the male world nor derived from it." An "objective" culture derived from this autonomous femininity would be "incommensurable on the basis of the standard of the male principle" and an "equivalency" could develop "… between two modes of existence that have a completely different rhythm …" He then questions this possibility by defining the nature of woman as homogeneous subjectivity centered on "being" as opposed to the male principle which proceeds beyond and develops outside itself, thus creating—or in his language, objectifying itself in forming culture. For women to objectify themselves would be antithetical to these defining features of femininity.

[[RunningHead]]

The Methods of Self Psychology

Michael Basch M.D.

Michael Basch

Michael Basch  

June 19, 1986

Dr. Philip Holzman, in his essay "Psychoanalysis: Is the Therapy Destroying the Science?" (This Journal, 33:725-770) says: "Kohut and the self psychology school insist that the methods of psychoanalysis are those of intuition, introspection and empathy" (p. 754). No citations or references in support of this assertion are given; the allegation is repeated on p. 755. In the references attached to his article, Dr. Holzman includes two books written by Kohut, The Analysis of the Self(1971), and The Restoration of the Self(1977); it is reasonable to assume that here one might find those statements by Kohut that support Dr. Holzman's indictment of self psychology. If it were indeed the case that Kohut and those who find his contributions to psychoanalysis useful subscribe to the idea that, in addition to empathy and introspection, intuition (defined by Webster's Seventh New Collegiate Dictionary as "la: immediate apprehension or cognition; lc: the power or faculty of attaining to direct knowledge or cognition without rational thought and inference") is basic to gathering the data from which self-psychologically oriented interpretations are made, then Holzman would be correct in his condemnation of self psychology as un- or antiscientific. However, Dr. Holzman's claim regarding self psychology is apparently based on a misreading of the text as Kohut, in the books that Dr. Holzman used to reach his conclusion, takes pains to definitely disassociate himself from both the notion that intuition can be equated with empathy, or that it can be relied upon by the psychoanalyst when gathering his or her data. The pertinent excerpts from these two books read: "Occasionally empathy is considered to be the equivalent of intuition, leading to the setting up of a spurious contrast between (a) sentimental and subjective (i.e., nonscientific)

[[RunningHead]]

Brain and Psyche: The Biology of the Unconscious

By Jonathan Winson. Garden City, N.Y.: Anchor Press/Doubleday, 1985, 300 pp., $16.95.

Robert Michels M.D.

Robert Michels

Robert Michels, M.D.
New York City

Robert Michels  

Jonathan Winson describes Freud's scientific style as consistently reaching "for the broad, encompassing theory," "ambitious," and "concerned with priority of discovery." He goes on to explain, "This constellation of characteristics was and is certainly not unusual in a scientist—it is the stuff from which many scientific discoveries are made. The danger, however, is that these characteristics may lead to the formulation of overgeneralized or otherwise incorrect hypotheses which may, for one reason or another, not be corrected by further observations" (pp. 85-86). Winson has some of these same characteristics. The jacket of his new book describes it as: "The first major effort to form a bridge between neuroscience and psychoanalysis," and Winson himself describes his goal as "… understanding how man's mental life is derived from the physical functioning of the brain" (p. 2).

Winson is Associate Professor of Neuroscience at Rockefeller University. His earlier publications were in journals such as Brain Research and Journal of Neurophysiology, and had titles such as "Gating of Neuronal Transmission in the Hippocampus: Efficacy of Transmission Varies with Behavioral State." He is friendly to psychoanalysis (He tells us that there has been "one major contribution" to "… the study of the psyche in its own right" (p. 3); and "no viable alternative theory has been suggested to explain the workings of the human psyche" [p. 4]). His program here is to dissect out the basic psychological discoveries of psychoanalysis, to describe some findings in modern neuroscience, and to use the latter to "… understand the biology underlying the psychological phenomena I have just described" (p. 160). Finally, remembering his concern about the pitfalls of Freud's style, he promises to tell us how his hypotheses might be tested by further observations (p. 7).

responseSet.title: Reply to Michael F. Basch
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Michael F. Basch

Philip Holzman Ph.D.

Philip Holzman

Philip Holzman  

September 11, 1986

Michael Basch takes strong objection to my statement that Heinz Kohut and the self psychology school "insist that the methods of psychoanalysis are those of intuition, introspection, and empathy," but he reserves his dudgeon only for my having linked intuition with introspection and empathy. I accept the correction that Kohut did not use the word "intuition" in describing his method, and that he went to some length to distinguish empathy from intuition. But my acceptance of this correction in no way invalidates the points I made in my article.

I argued that if one limits the psychoanalytic enterprise to the methods of introspection and empathy (I omit here the term "intuition," with no change in the argument), one eliminates the possibility to confirm or disconfirm most of its assertions. While I may have been textually incorrect in coupling empathy and introspection with intuition, I believe the logic of my argument is correct. By claiming introspection and empathy as the exclusive psychoanalytic methods, Kohut placed psychoanalysis squarely with the hermeneutic movement.

1011

[[RunningHead]]

Current Perspectives on Personality Disorders

Edited by James Frosch. Washington, DC: Amer. Psychiat. Press, 1983, 128 pp., $12.00.

Roger Mackinnon M.D.

Roger Mackinnon

Roger A. Mackinnon, M.D.
New York, NY

Roger Mackinnon  

The five chapters of this paperback book were originally presented as a symposium of the American Psychiatric Association on personality disorders in 1982. As would be expected, the book is aimed at a general psychiatric audience, yet it contains a significant amount of material of relevance for psychoanalysts—particularly those whose careers do not bring them into regular contact with biologically oriented psychiatrists.

The organization of the book reflects thoughtful choices on the part of the editor. A general background on the subject of character is provided in the first chapter by E. Auchincloss and R. Michels. They trace the evolution of Freud's views on character, then move into the contributions of other psychoanalytic theoreticians, and finally summarize the authors' understanding of the contemporary psychoanalytic view of character—all in 15 pages, written in clear, well-organized, nontechnical language! They comment on the lack of a cohesive psychoanalytic classification of character, referring to the conflicting systems of classification based on the drive and the developmental phase views of character (the oral or the anal character); the impulse-defense constellations (passive-aggressive personality); symptoms or behavioral configurations (the hysterical or obsessive character); a classification by theme ("the exceptions" or "those wrecked by success"); and a classification related to the functioning of a particular psychic structure (sociopathic characters or "criminals from a sense of guilt").

After summarizing the contributions of Freud, Abraham, Wilhelm Reich, Anna Freud, and Waelder, the authors move into the contemporary psychoanalytic view of character. They express a firm conviction of an underlying biological substrate that provides a matrix in which character develops. With regard to treatment, they state, "Character pathology is the primary and perhaps only indication for psychoanalytic treatment today. Symptoms are now viewed as clues to underlying conflicts and pathological character patterns seen as the most important manifestations of those conflicts. In fact, symptoms are often the lever for getting the patient involved in a treatment that has change of character as its primary goal" (p. 11).

[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Introductory Remarks

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro  

THE AMERICAN PSYCHOANALYTIC ASSOCIATION has just celebrated its seventy-fifth anniversary, and this year the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association has embarked on its thirty-fifth year of publication. It is now appropriate that we celebrate this forum for exchange of psychoanalytic ideas, research, and clinical findings that is currently the most widely circulated journal exclusively devoted to psychoanalysis in the world. We can well be proud!

Anniversaries invariably prompt us to look back at beginnings. Dr. John Frosch, the first editor of the Journal, has been kind enough to contribute a candid personal narrative history of its origins, which is also informative about our Association's middle years. His reminiscence describes the development from Bulletin to Journal. At that time, the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis and the Psychoanalytic Quarterly were already in existence, and were formidable competitive sources of psychoanalytic scholarship. Psychoanalysis continues to spawn new journals that grow out of the needs of practitioners, researchers, and new organizations. We welcome change and modernity, but we also can be pleased that the vision of John Frosch and the founding fathers of the Journal has created a viable and dominant force in the intellectual community devoted to psychoanalysis. We thank him and them.

To be only self-congratulatory, however, would miss the opportunity of a celebration which ought to look forward as well as be respectful of the past. Psychoanalysis continues to be a changing professional and intellectual discipline. It thrives on variation in themes presented in the Journal as well as changes among our authors. While we should honor and feature those who have become our dominant thinkers, we should always look for new creative minds.

This is a book remarkable for its scholarship and drama. Eissler offers us a study based on official documents heretofore unknown to the English reader, involving Freud's appearance before a parliamentary commission which met in the Spring of 1919 to inquire into malpractice charges against Professor Julius von Wagner-Jauregg, at that time Chairman of the Psychiatric Division of the Vienna University Hospital. He was eminent in psychiatric circles and later rose to international fame for his contribution to the prevention of goiter, advocating the use of iodized salt. In 1928 he won the Nobel Prize for his discovery of the treatment of progressive paralysis by malaria innoculation.

Some Contemporary Contributions to the Study of Emotions

Peter Knapp M.D.

Peter Knapp

Peter Knapp  

EMOTION—I USE THE TERM SYNONYMOUSLY with affect—is part of the fabric of psychoanalysis. Propositions about emotion saturate psychoanalytic writings, theoretical and technical. Yet psychoanalytic studies of the emotions remain fitful, largely unfocused. After 30 years, David Rapaport is still quoted: "We do not possess a systematic statement of the psychoanalytic theory of the affects"—this in spite of his powerful effort "to piece together the existing fragments of this theory" (1950).

Meanwhile, outside of psychoanalysis, a copious body of work has flourished, at times interpenetrating, at times running parallel, at times counter to psychoanalytic views. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of Anna O.: a Neuropsychiatric Perspective

Alison Orr-Andrawes M.D.

Alison Orr-Andrawes

Alison Orr-Andrawes, M.D.
500A East 875th Street
New York, NY 10128

Alison Orr-Andrawes  

ABSTRACT

Previous interpretations have not adequately explained the presence of focal neurological signs, delirium, and a variety of highly specific disturbances of perception, language, and sensorium in the case of Anna O. Examination of the neurological details suggests that Anna suffered from complex partial seizures exacerbated by drug dependence, and that she developed conversion symptoms patterned after the preexisting organic pathology. Hysterical conversion symptoms that mimic ictal events are not uncommon in psychomotor epilepsy. Recent neuropsychiatric models of hysteria are remarkably similar to the hypnoid states theory Breuer formulated, based on his observation of Anna O. The hypothesis presented here does not conflict with previous psychodynamic interpretations. I submit that it was the development of a conversion disorder patterned on actual organic phenomena, which highlighted for Breuer and Freud the "psychical mechanism" of somatic symptoms, thus paving the way for the important psychodynamic discoveries for which the case is remembered.

[[RunningHead]]

The Emotional Experience of Learning and Teaching

By Iscá Salzberger-Wittenberg, Gianna Henry, and Elsie Osborne. London, Boston, Melbourne & Henley: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1983, 154 pp., $10.95

Helen Beiser M.D.

Helen Beiser

Helen R. Beiser, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Helen Beiser  

This book was written for teachers at all levels by two psychotherapists at the Tavistock Clinic in London, and an educational psychologist at that institution. The theoretical background is largely Kleinian, but not overwhelmingly so. The Tavistock Clinic apparently offers a course for classroom teachers at all levels, from elementary through college. The scene moves back and forth from the experiences of the teachers in this course as students, to vignettes of their experiences as teachers with their students. The writers also tell of what they learn as they teach these teachers. There might be some question as to what value such a book might have for psychoanalysts, but it is as pertinent to teaching in a psychoanalytic institute as at some earlier level. I certainly have been impressed at how middle-aged psychiatrists can begin acting like adolescents as soon as they start formal training. Their resistances to learning material whose relevance is not immediately apparent can be as extreme as those of any high school class.

The first section, "Beginnings," deals sensitively with the anxieties and defenses of starting any new learning experience, and includes some basic psychoanalytic theory about separation anxiety and stranger anxiety. Effects of the environment as well as internal variables in students are considered, with some inferences as to how to reduce the stress for students entering a new learning experience.

The second section discusses the relationships of students and teachers. Teachers may be seen in a variety of roles—source of wisdom, comforter, object of envy, judge or authority. The author explains these distortions on the basis of transference, with individual examples. It could be particularly important for teachers of elementary level to understand the transference implications of pregnancy or illness on children who have experienced such events with parents. The next chapter reviews, in similar fashion, the teacher's expectations of students, sometimes equally unrealistic. Their own childhood experiences may influence how teachers perceive the teaching role. They may also experience their students in transference roles from siblings.

[[RunningHead]]

The Play of Transference: Some Reflections on Enactment in the Psychoanalytic Situation

James McLaughlin M.D.

James McLaughlin

James T. McLaughlin, M.D.
820 Devonshire Street
Pittsburgh, PA 15213

James McLaughlin  

ABSTRACT

The incessant play of nonverbal activity between patient and analyst actualizes and amplifies the primary verbal data of the psychoanalytic dialogue. Both parties must inevitably register this kinesic level of communication, and react with capacities acquired in and elaborated from earliest childhood. The analyst's apperceptive (unfocused) looking, as part of his freely hovering attentiveness, utilizes these capabilities gradually to perceive and organize patterns combining the verbal and nonverbal data. It is through the recognizing and eventual understanding of these gestalts that the analyst builds up his knowledge of his patients.

In these patterns can be identified: (a) conspicuous behaviors, idiosyncratic for the individual, which often yield to psychoanalytic inquiry to reveal their dynamic-historical antecedents; and (b) inconspicuous background kinesics, habitual to the individual, which ordinarily are opaque to analytic exploration, yet hold rich meaning. Observing these small behaviors in relation to verbal content provides evidence of their linkage to, and enactment of, pregenital-and genital-level conflicts over diadic and triadic object relations, even in highly structured personalities. These enactments combine elements of play, miming, and drama to constitute an experiential dimension that actualizes and externalizes the patients' inner life of conflict and relation to objects.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Concepts Useful in the Building of Political Foundations Between Nations: Track II Diplomacy

Vamik Volkan M.D.

Vamik Volkan

Vamik D. Volkan, M.D.
Blue Ridge Hospital University of Virginia Medical Center
Charlottesville, VA 22901

Vamik Volkan  

ABSTRACT

There are two phases in the process of negotiating for peace with antagonistic national groups. The leader (or leaders) of one such group will not negotiate with his opposite number until political foundations to support such negotiation are laid down, and obstacles to peace identified and dealt with; at least some measure of success is anticipated by both sides. Accordingly, the first phase requires the building of bridges between those representing opposing nations. The activities in this preliminary phase are sometimes called Track II diplomacy; they differ from those of official or Track I diplomacy. I suggest two kinds of concepts that may be useful in Track II diplomacy, and present them in the hope of initiating further discussion, and exploration of metapsychological underpinnings of each concept. As Mitscherlich (1971) suggested, the psychology of international politics may be better understood if psychoanalysts are willing to engage in interdisciplinary work with others engaged in such endeavors.

[[RunningHead]]

Implications for the Structural Model of Freud's Assumptions about Perception

Joseph Slap M.D.

Joseph Slap

Joseph W. Slap, M.D.
1601 Walnut Street (Room 1312)
Philadelphia, PA 19102

Joseph Slap  

ABSTRACT

Freud made assumptions about perception that strongly influenced his final model of the mind, the structural model. Two of these assumptions, (1) that perception is exclusively a function of the ego and (2) that perception is an accurate, veridical representation of external reality, are questioned on the basis of clinical findings and current conceptualizations. Changes are suggested, namely, that we grant that the repressed has a capacity for perception and recognize that perception is not an inherent ability for exact, photographic registration of the external world, but that it is a function of the mind mediated through external receptors and the central nervous system. Perception is dependent on mental organizations (schemas) which are affected by past experience, psychodynamics, affective states, and cognitive style. These changes produce a version of the structural model which is congruent with a schema model previously advanced as a competing model. It is argued that this version of the structural model has significant clinical and theoretical advantages over previous models.

[[RunningHead]]

Transference Neurosis as a Waking Dream: Notes on a Clinical Enigma

James Kern M.D.

James Kern

James W. Kern, M.D.
2200 Fuller Road
Ann Arbor, MI 48105

James Kern  

ABSTRACT

This paper demonstrates clinically that the interactional features of a transference neurosis are the waking equivalents of a manifest dream. Through analytic investigation of the emerging repetitive extraverbal elements of apparent transference resistance behavior, it is discovered that the systematic analysis of the details of such behavior yields a picture of synthetic construction fundamentally the same as that seen in dreams. By using Freud's technique of systematic dream interpretation, the tightly organized, coded, and camouflaged presence of many key compromise formations determining a neurosis are found to be represented in compact, highly condensed clinical interactions, providing an overall picture of dreamwork in action. The four components of dreamwork are found to be the principal means by which the unconscious genetic and dynamic material is represented in the analytic field.

Theodore Jacobs M.D. and Judith Chused M.D.

Theodore J. Jacobs, M.D.

1805 Randolph Street, N.W.

Judith Chused  

THEODORE JACOBS BEGAN THE PANEL with a description of a twenty-three-year-old patient who sought treatment because of neurotic fears, obsessional worries, and inability to choose an independent way of life. This patient, the Wolf Man, was one of the many young adults and late adolescents whom Freud treated. In essence, their clinical material provided the foundation for the theory and technique of psychoanalysis. Nonetheless, as Jacobs pointed out, little specific attention has been directed to the problems of the young adult (18–25) as distinct from those of the adolescent or mature adult. This panel is the first in the history of the American Psychoanalytic Association to do so.

Jacobs then raised several questions for the panelists to consider: Are there therapeutically relevant subphases within young adulthood? What are the significant differences, if any, faced by men and women in this age group? How do current societal and cultural issues influence young adults as they interact with the compromise formations that are solutions to internal conflicts?

Jacobs spoke of the developmental tasks of the young adult—the consolidation of ego capacities that is required for life and career decisions, referring to Adatto's conceptualization of the changes that take place during this period: (1) movement from the narcissistic self-involvement of adolescence to more intimate and enduring object relations; (2) integration of sexual development with other personality changes, with the firm establishment of sexual identity, and the formation of an irreversible sexual position; (3) development of greater breadth of ego functions with behavior less governed by conflict (as adolescent conflicts are reduced in intensity) and more energy available for new interests and activities, with greater tolerance of frustration (character, as we have come to understand that term, being

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 23, 1984.

Panelists: Sander M. Abend, Peter Blos, Sr., Ethel S. Person, Melvin A. Scharfman; Discussant: Samuel Ritvo.

Toward the Further Understanding of Homosexual Women

Adrienne Applegarth M.D. and Abby Wolfson Ph.D.

Abby Wolfson

Adrienne Applegarth, M.D.
Abby Wolfson, Ph.D.
419 Locust Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Abby Wolfson  

ADRIENNE APPLEGARTH INTRODUCED THE PANEL by defining its task as presenting observations and points of view rather than providing a definitive revision of psychoanalytic theory about female homosexuality. Our explanations of homosexuality in women rest on our theories of female sexual development, some of which remain troubling to many analysts. In particular, three aspects of psychoanalytic theory central to the subject of female homosexuality are believed to be inadequate by many analysts: the establishment of gender identity, sexual object choice, and the urge toward motherhood. We would therefore do well to collect more observational data about female development.

Classical psychoanalytic theory, as outlined by Freud, holds that libido is basically masculine and that the girl develops first in a masculine direction, only later discovering her vagina. However, this is not the same as the establishment of gender identity, which Freud seemed to take for granted. Both Stoller's work and that of the Money group suggest that gender identity is irreversibly established by the age of eighteen months—before the discovery of the genital difference—and depends almost exclusively on parental influence rather than on physical, gene-determined sex. Gender identity is based on early identifications; if they are disrupted, gender identity may be disturbed.

The classical explanation of sexual object choice assumes that the girl's first love object in the oedipal phase is the mother. When her homosexual wishes are disappointed, she reacts by turning to the father. A different view is that the girl, like the boy, enters both positive and negative oedipal phases together.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, San Diego, CA, May 4, 1984.

[[RunningHead]]

Fact and Fantasy in the Seduction Theory: A Historical Review

Jean Schimek Ph.D.

Jean Schimek

Jean G. Schimek, Ph.D.
Department of Psychology New York University
6 Washington Place
New York, NY 1003

Jean Schimek  

ABSTRACT

This article surveys Freud's various versions of the seduction theory, from 1896 to 1933. It is concluded that the seduction theory had never been based on the patients' direct statements and conscious recall of seduction by the father in early childhood—unlike what Freud was to state much later (1933). This early seduction was mostly reconstructed by Freud from the patient's verbal material and behavior in treatment (including memories of sexual experiences from later childhood) which he interpreted as disguised and incomplete "reproductions" and reenactments of the original seduction trauma.

Further, the external trauma was never meant to account by itself for the later neurotic symptoms. The "delayed action" of its unconscious memory, producing the repression of an event from the time of puberty, was a necessary part of the process. Thus internal psychic transformations and conflicts, anticipating Freud's later emphasis on fantasy and psychic reality, were already an intrinsic part of the seduction etiology of 1896. It is also noted that the father played no central role in the original theory as presented in 1896; it is only in the letters to Fliess that the father emerged as the prime seducer.

The implications of this clarification of the seduction theory for the understanding of the changes and continuity in the development of Freud's theories are highlighted; their relevance to ongoing issues in psychoanalysis about the role of external trauma, fantasy, and reconstruction are briefly examined.

[[RunningHead]]

Changes in Psychoanalytic Ideas: Transference Interpretation

Arnold Cooper M.D.

Arnold Cooper

Arnold M. Cooper, M.D.
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021

Arnold Cooper  

ABSTRACT

Interpretation of the transference is central to all psychoanalytic models. Definitions of transference and transference interpretation have changed greatly during the past half-century, influenced by major movements in philosophy, advances in psychoanalytic research and theory, and changes in our understanding of Freud. This paper suggests that historical, relatively simple, concepts of the transference as the reproduction in the present of significant relationships from the past do not adequately meet current clinical and theoretical demands. Modernist views of the transference emphasize as additional sources of transference responses, the role of the analytic background of safety, the constant modification of unconscious fantasy and internal representations, and the interactive nature of transference responses, with important interpersonal and intersubjective components. It is suggested that the evolving modernist views of transference and transference interpretation permit a fuller accounting for transference phenomena and open the way for better informed interventions. A brief discussion of the issue of psychological "truth" and "distortion" as applied to transference phenomena is presented. The themes are illustrated with clinical vignettes.

The Role of Reality Testing in Psychoanalysis: Followup of 22 Cases

Judy Kantrowitz Ph.D. , Ann Katz , Frank Paolitto M.D. , Jerome Sashin M.D. and Leonard Solomon Ph.D.

Leonard Solomon

Judy L. Kantrowitz, Ph.D.
Ann L. Katz, Ed.D.
Frank Paolitto, M.D.
Jerome Sashin, M.D.
Leonard Solomon, Ph.D.
334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02146

Leonard Solomon  

ABSTRACT

As part of a prospective, longitudinal study of psychoanalytic outcome, 22 patients were evaluated for changes in their level of reality testing. These patients had been accepted for supervised psychoanalysis with candidates in training by senior analysts who had diagnosed them as neurotic. Psychological tests given prior to the beginning of analysis indicated, however, that more than one third of our sample demonstrated serious distortions in their perceptions of reality. When psychological tests administered one year after the completion of analysis were compared with these pretreatment tests, no significant improvement was found in the level of reality testing for the group as a whole. However, when excluding two patients who had suffered major traumas in the year following psychoanalysis, significant improvement in reality testing was found for the remaining 20 patients. Post-treatment interviews with the treating analysts revealed that in the course of analysis itself, approximately one third of these patients showed some disturbance in reality testing; only three of these patients showed significant improvement in the level of their reality testing during treatment. These findings suggest that while patients may not present clinically with evidence of impaired reality testing, such evidence may be available in psychological testing, and may become apparent in the course of the treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

Rules of Clinical Understanding in Classical Psychoanalysis and in Self Psychology: A Comparison

Gail Reed Ph.D.

Gail Reed

Gail S. Reed, Ph.D.
1199 Park Avenue (Suite 1G)
New York, NY 10128

Gail Reed  

ABSTRACT

This paper compares the rules that govern clinical understanding in classical psychoanalysis and in self psychology. The rules of understanding in the two systems are fundamentally different. Self psychology interprets causes and relies on interpretive rules similar to those used in allegory. Classical psychoanalysis investigates reasons and seeks to uncover the patient's own interpretive system. Self psychology refinds its own theory in the relation between elements disguised in the manifest content of patients' productions. Classical psychoanalysis seeks to open up manifest content. Where self psychology reiterates theory in its interpretations, classical interpretations move away from theoretical generalizations toward specific fantasy-memory constellations. Theory, in self psychology, organizes directly the content of interpretations; theory in classical psychoanalysis organizes a technique of exploration and furnishes general sequences according to which data can be understood. This paper also suggests that the differences in the rules of clinical understanding have potential consequences for psychoanalysis which manifest themselves in psychoanalytic training and in the capacity for self-analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Projection and Projective Identification: Developmental and Clinical Aspects

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605

Otto Kernberg  

ABSTRACT

Projective identification and projection are defined, described, and contrasted. Projective identification is seen as an early or primitive defensive operation, and projection as later or more advanced and derivative in nature. The developmental origins and adaptive functions of projective identification are examined with an emphasis on the cognitive preconditions for the operation of this defense. The varying functions of both defensive operations are described within the context of psychotic, borderline, and neurotic personality organization.

Case material is presented to illustrate the diagnostic approach to and the clinical functions of projective identification, particularly its importance in contributing to complementary identification in the countertransference. Also illustrated is the technical management of severe transference regression under the impact of projective identification. Finally, alternative approaches to the diagnosis and interpretation of projective identification are discussed.

Changes in the Level and Quality of Object Relations in Psychoanalysis: Followup of a Longitudinal, Prospective Study

Judy Kantrowitz Ph.D. , Ann Katz , Frank Paolitto M.D. , Jerome Sashin M.D. and Leonard Solomon Ph.D.

Leonard Solomon

Judy L. Kantrowitz, Ph.D.
Ann L. Katz, Ed.D.
Frank Paolitto, M.D.
Jerome Sashin, M.D.
Leonard Solomon, Ph.D.
334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02146

Leonard Solomon  

ABSTRACT

As part of a prospective, longitudinal study of suitability for and outcome of psychoanalysis, 22 patients were evaluated for changes in the level and quality of their object relations. These patients had been accepted for supervised analysis with candidates in training by senior analysts who had diagnosed them as neurotic. The majority of these patients reported difficulties in relationships as at least one of their reasons for originally seeking psychoanalytic treatment. Prior to beginning analysis, each patient was given a battery of psychological tests. One year after the termination of analysis, the test battery was readministered and a followup interview was conducted separately with each patient and analyst. Ratings of change based on patient and analyst interviews and comparisons of psychological tests before and after treatment all showed statistically significant improvement in the level and quality of object relations for this patient group. The results of this study support what is often observed in clinical practice, that psychoanalysis has a positive effect on the level and quality of a patient's object relations.

[[RunningHead]]

The Emergence of Assertion and Aggression During Infancy: A Psychoanalytic Systems Approach

Gerald Stechler Ph.D. and Antonia Halton Ph.D.

Antonia Halton

Gerald Stechler, Ph.D.
Charles River Hospital
203 Grove Street
Wellesley, MA 02181
Antonia Halton, Ph.D.

Antonia Halton  

ABSTRACT

A model is presented in which assertion and aggression are seen as arising from two entirely different biopsychological systems in the human infant. Assertion derives from the universal tendency to be active, to seek stimuli, to generate plans, and to carry them out. It is a self-activating system and is generally associated with the positive affects of joy, interest, and excitement. Aggression derives from the equally universal self-protective system. This system, however, is reactive in the face of a perceived threat and is associated with the dysphoric affects of anger, fear, and distress.

During the infant and toddler period, the child's assertions may be treated punitively by the parents as if they were aggressive acts. The child in turn feels threatened and becomes self-protective. The result is a contamination between assertion and aggression and the creation of a new self-sustaining system in which aggression appears to be spontaneous and self-activating. If assertions are frequently blocked, the contamination may be extensive, and it may appear as if an aggressive instinctual process is present. However, a systemic explanation provides a much better fit with the empirical observations than does an instinctual one.

Four examples of different family styles are presented. The model permits one to understand the subtle qualitative differences in style reflecting different individual combinations of assertion and aggression and their associated affects.

[[RunningHead]]

The Manifest Dream in Psychoanalysis: A Clarification

Sydney Pulver M.D.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney E. Pulver, M.D.
111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139

Sydney Pulver  

ABSTRACT

Controversies about the value of the manifest dream in psychoanalysis are usually a matter of semantics. The author concludes that two clinically meaningful questions about the manifest dream can be asked: (1) Is the patient's report of a dream useful in formulating an interpretation if no formal associations to the dream elements are given? Inasmuch as such reports always include some kind of associations, and the analyst always possesses considerable knowledge about the patient, the answer to this question would seem to be "Yes, at least at times." (2) In what ways can the manifest dream contribute to our understanding of the dream and the dreamer? Both the literature and clinical experience indicate that there are many ways; these are summarized in an appendix. One approach, the direct decoding of manifest dream elements, is discussed in some detail. Several examples of the undisguised appearance of memories of childhood traumatic experiences in the manifest dream are presented.

Conversion of Psychotherapy to Psychoanalysis

Jerome Oremland M.D. and Charles Fisher M.D.

Charles Fisher

Jerome D. Oremland, M.D.
Charles P. Fisher, M.D.
405 Locust Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Charles Fisher  

IN HIS OPENING REMARKS, OREMLAND RAISED the central issue of the Panel—whether conversion from psychotherapy to psychoanalysis requires transfer of the patient to another analyst. The more historical and conservative view was that a new analyst is required. The idea that a change in analysts is unnecessary is emerging in recent years as the result of significant conceptual shifts concerning the relation between psychotherapy and psychoanalysis.

Oremland described a spectrum of viewpoints regarding the distinctions among the analytic psychotherapies and psychoanalysis proper. The historical dichotomy between exploratory and supportive psychotherapy has conceptual ambiguities in that it leaves unclear what is to be explored and what is to be supported. By and large, noninterpretive interventions, whether exploratory or supportive, are varieties of suggestion. A different dimension, introduced by R. R. Greenson, differentiated "those psychotherapies which use the transference toward therapeutic goals from those, of which analysis proper is the most pure form, in which the transference … is the central object of study."

Oremland stated that the panelists vary in their views of the importance of interpretation in psychotherapy—one pole being the viewpoint that psychotherapy utilizes selective and partial interpretations, along with interactive interventions; the other pole being the viewpoint that "psychotherapy for a wide variety of patients can be comprised of predominantly interpretive interventions, similar in mode and manner to the activity of the analyst in analysis."

He asked the panelists to discuss "the qualitative and quantitative range of interaction that can constitute the initial psychotherapy" and still preserve the potential for subsequent conversion.

To the Panel at large, and to Ernst Ticho in particular, he addressed

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 16, 1983.

Panelists: Leonard Horwitz, Ernst A. Ticho, Otto F. Kernberg, Merton M. Gill.

Psychoanalytic Contributions to Psychiatric Nosology: Forms of Masochistic Behavior

Richard Simons M.D.

Richard Simons

Richard C. Simons, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry University of Colorado Health Sciences Center
4200 East Ninth Avenue
Denver, CO 80262

Richard Simons  

ABSTRACT

This paper summarizes the major strengths and weaknesses of current psychiatric nosology, and then discusses various forms of masochistic behavior as one illustration of how psychoanalysis can contribute to the continuing evolution of psychiatric nosology. Masochistic behavior is separated into behavior in which there is a predominantly conscious link between pleasure and pain, and behavior in which that link is predominantly unconscious. Both categories are further separated into normal and pathological forms. The current diagnostic category of dysthymic disorder is then discussed, with consideration given to the contribution that psychoanalysis can make toward the clarification and delineation of this heterogeneous diagnostic category.

[[RunningHead]]

The Fundamental Rule: A Study of Current Usage

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D. and Floyd Galler M.D.

Floyd Galler

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Floyd B. Galler, M.D.
1616 Eighteenth Street, N.W.
Washington, DC 20009

Floyd Galler  

ABSTRACT

Members of the editorial boards of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Psychoanalytic Quarterly, and Psychoanalytic Inquiry, plus selected analysts from Canada, France, and England, were asked how they presented the fundamental rule to their analysands and what considerations led to their choice of initial guidelines. Forty-nine of eighty-three analysts responded to the survey.

The respondents described diversity in phrasing of the fundamental rule, the time in analysis when the guidelines are given, and the reasons for the practice they follow. The method each chose appears to reflect the differing conceptions analysts have of the analytic process. Two major trends emerged: either our respondents emphasized delineating a work or functional framework or contract, or they emphasized establishing an attitude or spirit of cooperation, mutuality and work sharing. In each group respondents differed as to whether they favored minimal or more lengthy instructions. They also differed as to whether they followed a consistent approach or one tailored to the needs of the individual analysand. A source of tension lay in an inclination either to remain in the tradition set by Freud or to experiment with different phrasings and timings. The authors give their reasons for choosing to give explicit instructions, requesting associational material beyond thoughts and feelings, and describing resistance as inevitable and a potential source of insight.

Heiman Van Dam M.D.

Heiman Van Dam

Heiman Van Dam, M.D.
941 Westwood Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90024

Heiman Van Dam  

ABSTRACT

This paper describes an example of acting out of countertransference during a brief but unexpected serious illness. My emphasis is on the regression that occurred with hospitalization and how it dovetailed with a particular patient's transference. A confluence of my residual neurosis, the patient's neurosis, her transference state, her other characteristics (that she was female, for example), plus the fact that she was the last patient contacted, all conspired with the regression from the trauma of being hospitalized to produce the countertransference reaction.

This presentation is also intended to add to the very sparse literature on a distasteful subject of supreme importance.

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association: a Retrospective (1953–1972)

John Frosch M.D.

John Frosch

John Frosch, M.D.
4 Chester Drive
Great Neck, NY 11021

John Frosch  

ABSTRACT

In this article, as Editor Emeritus, I review the early evolution of the Journal. It began with the Bulletin, of which I was Editor. It became necessary to give more attention and space to the publication of group discussions and panels.

The early days of the Journal were characterized at times by internecine strife of a personal, political, and scientific nature. My ability to deal with this situation was hampered by my lack of knowledge, and even an amateurish quality concerned with editing a journal, although I had had considerable experience in editing The Annual Survey of Psychoanalysis.

Out of the realization of the impact of this strife on the scientific activities of the Journal, grew the firm determination to emphasize the role of the Editor and the Journal as free from the political fluctuations that characterized the Association from time to time. This was essential in establishing freedom from pressure, and autonomy insofar as the scientific activities of the Journal were concerned. It was indicated especially in setting up the criteria for selection of papers, so as to create a climate of fairness and objectivity. These criteria are stated in the article.

How the various problems mentioned above were dealt with is described in this contribution.

Therapeutic Strategy and Psychoanalytic Technique

Steven Levy M.D.

Steven Levy

Steven T. Levy, M.D.
Emory University Psychoanalytic clinic
1711 Uppergate Drive, N. E.
Atlanta, GA 30322

Steven Levy  

ABSTRACT

The role of therapeutic strategy within psychoanalytic technique is described. An antistrategic bias inherent in certain aspects of the "classical" technique is explored in relation to the historical development of psychoanalysis. Clinical expertise, which includes the making of strategic or tactical choices, is relegated to the "unofficial," due in part to this negative bias impeding the study of technical differences in favor of general agreement about a theory of technique that may differ considerably from actual clinical work. A case is presented that illustrates strategic choices in the management of a severe character resistance in a supervised analysis. Some consequences of a negative bias against therapeutic strategy as it relates to psychoanalytic training is described.
