[[RunningHead]]

The Borderline and Severely Neurotic Child

Peter Blos M.D. and Robert Galatzer-Levy M.D.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Peter Blos, Jr., M.D.
Robert M. Galatzer-Levy, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, ILL 60601

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

BLOS OPENED THE PANEL, RECALLING that childhood borderline states were first recognized in work with severely disturbed, nonpsychotic inpatients. These children had circumscribed ego malformations and disturbed object relations. Many had primary ego apparatus deficits. Successful analytic work with "better functioning" borderlines and severely neurotic children focused interest on the psychogenesis of borderline states. Yet the definition of the term "borderline" as applied to children remains unclear. Pine's description of the children's stable instability, predictable inappropriateness, and stable oddness is as close as we come to defining the disorder. This, and a forthcoming panel, will attempt to explore the meaning of borderline states in children, to characterize their self- and narcissistic pathology, difficulties with body image, gender identity, object relations, superego defects, defensive structure, and drive organization. Is there a subpopulation of borderline children for whom analysis is the treatment of choice? How are borderline states in children and adults related?

Paulina Kernberg described the psychiatric findings in borderline children. Borderline personality organization is associated with several childhood syndromes. Conduct disorders and attention deficit disorders with hyperactivity are common. Other presentations include separation anxiety; overanxious; schizoid; identity, eating, developmental, and language disorders; and elective mutism. Although checklists may be used to diagnose borderline personality disorder, Kernberg underlined three primary characteristics of the illness: (1) a disturbed sense of identity; (2) sudden shifts in level of functioning, and (3) relative inability to accept responsibility for one's actions. Disturbed identity sense is the most important. "Identity" does not have the same dimensions as in adults, but is applicable to children to indicate that they normally know what they can do, what they are like, and what they like.

Objects and Attitudes

Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton, M.D.
1081 Moraga Drive
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Allan Compton  

ABSTRACT

The concept of an attitudinal object has long been present in psychoanalysis but almost entirely neglected. This omission is integral in the confusion and controversy about the object concept in general. It has left a theoretical gap which the so-called object relations theories have attempted to fill. This paper offers some preliminary suggestions for a psychoanalytic understanding of attitudes as a first level of inference in the study of relationships and their p psychological basis.

Illness in the Doctor: Implications for the Psychoanalytic Process

Harvey Schwartz M.D.

Harvey Schwartz

Harvey J. Schwartz, M.D.
Jefferson Medical College
1015 Walnut Street (Suite 327 G)
Philadelphia, PA 19107

Harvey Schwartz  

ABSTRACT

The meaning and impact of serious illness in the therapist is described as it affects the doctor's intrapsychic analytic space, the analyzing field, and the fantasy life of patients. The brief literature on this topic is reviewed with particular reference to what patients should be told about one's illness. The author's experience of serious illness is described as it related to his reaction to his work with patients. Patient material is presented that demonstrates the utility of an abstinent stance even when encountering real and disturbing events. Freud's experience with long-term disability is recalled and the special circumstances posed by major illness for the analytic relationship are discussed. The unparalleled experience provided the patient by one's delicate neutrality at such times reveals to him the true depth of unconscious fantasy and its lasting influence on adult functioning.

[[RunningHead]]

The Development of Time Sense: From Object Constancy to Adolescence

Calvin Colarusso M.D.

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin A. Colarusso, M.D.
1020 Prospect Street (Suite 415 A)
La Jolla, CA 92037

Calvin Colarusso  

ABSTRACT

A developmental line of time sense during the oedipal phase and latency is described. Subjective time sense is seen as constantly evolving, shaped by the emergence of cognitive capacities, significant emotional themes, and environmental influences. Temporal development during these phases is unique because the capacity to tell objective time is present for the first time. Significant emotional influences are the Oedipus complex, infantile neurosis, and superego formation. The concepts of Mother Time and Father Time are presented as useful descriptors of the child's intrapsychic representation of the chief environmental effectors of temporal organization. Clinical examples demonstrate that the development of time sense can be unaffected, impeded, or incorporated into psychopathology.

Issues in Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Borderline/severely Neurotic Child

Peter Blos M.D. and Robert Galatzer-Levy M.D.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Peter Blos, Jr., M.D.
Robert Galatzer-Levy, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

THIS PANEL IS A SEQUEL TO AN EARLIER PANEL which centered on diagnosis and pathology.1 It focuses on therapy, studying a case in detail.

The case of eleven-year-old Matthew was presented by Marans. The child was well known to the Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic. His six-year-old adoptive brother was in analysis, his mother had been analyzed, and he himself was observed by a social worker during his first two years, so an unusual amount of information was available about his early development.

His mother said he was clingy, alternately demanding and withdrawn. She hounded him to do simple tasks. He had no age-appropriate interests. Sometimes mother was provoked to physical violence, which terrified mother and child. Matthew wanted treatment. He felt sad and missed his father who left the family when Matthew was eight, and lived abroad.

Matthew's parents, whose social backgrounds were completely different, married when his mother was in her late thirties and father in his early twenties. Father was delinquent, sporadically employed, and a heavy drinker. Labor and delivery were prolonged and painful. The mother suffered both breast and vaginal infections, but insisted that nursing be Matthew's sole nourishment. The baby slept poorly and cried incessantly for two months, until mother was convinced to add supplemental feedings.

The infant observer reported that despite mother's severe depression, Matthew seemed alert and happy during his first two years, once he was adequately fed. Mother worried, as she worried about herself, that he was defective or might grow fat. At four months Matthew first refused the breast, then fed well.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Denver, Colorado, May 18, 1985.

Presenter: Steven Marans; Discussants: Jack Novick, Henry Parens, Alan Zients.

1See J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn., 35(1).727

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Observations on the Effect of Lithium on Manic Attacks

Felix Loeb M.D. and Loretta Loeb M.D.

Loretta Loeb

Felix F. Loeb, Jr., M.D.
Loretta R. Loeb, M.D.
1908 S.W. Terrace Drive
Portland, OR 97201

Loretta Loeb  

ABSTRACT

This paper describes a predictable relation between our manicdepressive patients' blood lithium levels and particular changes in their conscious and unconscious mental processes (i.e., their thoughts, wishes, fantasies, inclinations, and feelings). These changes were, in turn, predictively related to specific changes in these patients' overt manic symptomatology.

Because each of our patients' manic episodes was heralded by a marked increase in unconscious or conscious phallic sexual thoughts, feelings, and behaviors, and because this increase preceded any observed deterioration in ego or superego functioning, we hypothesize that a primary increase in our patients' phallic instinctual drives secondarily overwhelmed the capacity of their egos to defend against these drives, and that this, in turn, resulted in the development of our patients' overt manic symptoms.

Psychoanalysis (or psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy) made our patients consciously aware both of their previously unconscious phallic sexual thoughts and impulses, and of their defenses against them. This new awareness enabled our patients to recognize when their, now conscious, phallic sexual impulses and thoughts became inappropriately intensified; and this, in turn, permitted them to avoid overt manic episodes by counteracting these inappropriate inclinations with increased doses of lithium.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

René Magritte: Coping with Loss—Reality and Illusion

Milton Viederman M.D.

Milton Viederman

Milton Viederman, M.D.
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021

Milton Viederman  

ABSTRACT

René Magritte was profoundly influenced in his painterly style and in the content of many of his paintings by his mother's suicide when he was fourteen. This paper explores the influence of this traumatic event on his work. My thesis is that the nighttime suicide of Magritte's mother by drowning: (1) had a profound impact on the form and style of his paintings; (2) his paintings represented an effort, mostly successful, to contain and master through intellectual control with isolation of affect the trauma of this experience, which must at the time have evoked helplessness and despair; (3) the contents of a series of his paintings directly reconstruct the traumatic experience under control and in the service of mastery; and (4) there are paintings, mostly painted at the time he clearly developed his own style (1926-1930), that reveal a breakthrough of projected primitive aggressive impulses and unconscious representations of the aggressive mother. In these latter paintings, both form and content are dramatically different from the main corpus of Magritte's work.

[[RunningHead]]

The Consciousness of Being Conscious

Edward Joseph M.D.

Edward Joseph

Edward D. Joseph, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Mount Sinai School of Medicine
1 East 100th Street
New York, NY 10029

Edward Joseph  

ABSTRACT

Starting with the therapeutic advantage gained when insight acquires consciousness, an investigation of the nature and function of consciousness is undertaken. Consciousness is a state of awareness, having a range of higher mental functions serving a regulatory, controlling, and integrating role in mental activity. There are high levels of thinking, reality testing, experiencing, judging, anticipating; self-awareness and self-reflection enter into these controlling activities. Psychoanalysis has rightly been a science that studies the workings and contents of the unconscious portions of the mind. It has perhaps overlooked the important role that consciousness plays in ordinary life and in providing the levels of control and self-awareness individuals both experience and require. That pathology and disturbances of function may accompany normal states of consciousness as well as altered states of consciousness is a common clinical phenomenon. Psychoanalysis as a therapy widens the scope of the conscious control systems.

Psychoanalytic Contributions to Psychiatric Nosology

Robert Wallerstein M.D. and Morris Peltz M.D.

Morris Peltz

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Morris L. Peltz, M.D.
3905 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Morris Peltz  

IN INTRODUCING THE PANEL, THE CHAIRMAN noted the difficulties DSM-III poses for the psychoanalyst. Because DSM-III attempts to be descriptively precise and avowedly atheoretical, there may be no easy overlap between the needs of organized psychiatry and the needs of the practicing psychoanalyst.

Wallerstein cited two cogent reasons for beginning with "Psychoanalytic Nosology: An Historical Review." First, it is critical to understand that a dynamic conception of nosology is "an ever-changing and a constructed corpus" contingent on expanding clinical and theoretical knowledge. Second, nosology and diagnosis achieve relevance only when considered in the overall context of case formulation, treatment indications, treatability, and treatment planning.

Wallerstein began with a review of Freud's lifetime interest in formulating a psychoanalytic nosology. In the Studies on Hysteria (S.E., 2, p. VII; S.E., 3, p. 159), Freud distinguished three varieties of hysteria: hypnoid, retention, and defense, later collapsed as a "neurosis of defense" (S.E., 3, p. 47). In his first nosological paper, "The Neuro-psychoses of Defense" (1894), Freud defined three distinct entities; conversion hysteria, obsessional neurosis, and hallucinatory psychosis, which represented different attempts to cope with or defend against an incompatible idea, or set of ideas. In conversion hysteria, the affect, split from incompatible idea, enters the somatic realm. In obsessional neurosis, the split-off affect remains in the psychic sphere but attaches itself to ideas not otherwise incompatible creating "false connections" which are the obsessional ideas. In psychosis, the incompatible idea is avoided by a flight into psychosis.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Denver, Colorado, May 18, 1985.

Panelists: Sol Altshul, Robert Michels, Richard C. Simons.

[[RunningHead]]

Drives, Affects, Behavior—And Learning: Approaches to a Psychobiology of Emotion and to an Integration of Psychoanalytic and Neurobiologic Thought

Andrew Schwartz M.D.

Andrew Schwartz

Andrew Schwartz, M.D.
Barton Hall
St. Elizabeths Hospital
Washington, DC 20032

Andrew Schwartz  

ABSTRACT

Drawing on concepts and data from psychoanalysis, neuroscience, ethology, and experimental psychology, this paper suggests that affects are the prime movers of human motivation and have, in addition, these properties: (1) they are neurophysiologically generated, sensationlike signals origination not in classical sensory systems, but in limbic structures; (2) activation of the neurobiologic affect generators reliably triggers motor stereotypes—like facial expression, posture, and tone of voice—which in turn transmit the physical data underlying empathy and emotional communication; and (3) appetitively sought pleasant emotions-sensations and aversive dysphoric feelings serve respectively as the ultimate, "brain-synthesized" positive and negative reinforcers of emotional learning.

The latter ideas point to derivative hypotheses: that parental nonverbal behavior and communication evoke in children the affects that guide character development and choice of defense, and that these processes are consistent with an affect-reinforced associative learning model comprising both operant and classical conditioning paradigms.

Psychoanalysis as therapy, then, may mechanistically rely for its clinical effectiveness on extinctionlike processes in which new experience in the analyst-patient relationship leads to the modulation or extinction of initially crippling signal affects associatively linked to various other affects, appetites, and behaviors.

Finally, the core hypotheses mesh both with recent findings from the cellular study of affect and learning mechanisms and with associative learning-unlearning assumptions variably explicit in psychoanalytic writings since the time of Freud's (1895) Project.

Reconstruction of an Early Seduction and its Aftereffects

Miriam Williams M.D.

Miriam Williams

Miriam Williams, M.D.
304 24th Street
Santa Monica, CA 90402

Miriam Williams  

ABSTRACT

This clinical presentation deals with the aftereffects, in a male patient, of a severely disturbed rapprochement phase, marked by many separations and a molestation before the age of three. The patient dreamed repeatedly of violence, often directed against himself, but the actual molestation was sealed off by formidable repression. A first analysis, with a male analyst, produced no relief from the patient's depression, crippling insomnia, and inability to form an intimate relationship. The second analysis progressed only when the repetitive dreams were understood to be repressed experiences of seduction and molestation.

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, Vol. 39

Edited by Albert J. Solnit, Ruth S. Eissler, and Peter B. Neubauer. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1984, 691 pp., $40.00.

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
Great Neck, NY

Jules Glenn  

This volume contains, in addition to the customary sections, a moving memorial to Anna Freud. Her death in 1982 created a serious hiatus in psychoanalysis as a theory and body of knowledge. Reading the articles in her memory by Joseph Goldstein, Peter B. Neubauer, Leo Rangel, Albert J. Solnit, Lottie M. Newman and Robert S. Wallerstein, one recalls with awe the wide range of her vast contributions as well as her personal influence in stimulating research and clarification and expansion of theory. Nobody has contributed so much to psychoanalytic conceptualization, research methodology and its implementation, child, adolescent, and adult psychoanalytic technique, and the application of psychoanalytic knowledge to law, education, child rearing, residential treatment, treatment and prevention of childhood disorders, to mention some of the outstanding areas.

The 26 articles in the "usual" sections (of which I will discuss only 11 because of space limitations) reflect the high standards we have come to expect of the Psychoanalytic Study of the Child.

A number of articles in the Theory and Development section emphasize instinctual aspects of the personality. T. Wayne Downey attempts to conceptualize aggression as a positive constructive force as well as a negative destructive one. Aggression, he says (p. 134), commences "with a state of primary omnipotent illusion fueled by aggression." It "functions to differentiate self from love objects." The transitional object "knits together aggressive and libidinal drive elements in a preliminary attempt to establish safe, enduring object relations" and leads to the achievement of object constancy.
